skill.name: tdd-vitest-typescript
skill.description: Guide test-driven TypeScript development with Vitest, including red-green-refactor workflows, test organization, mocking patterns, coverage guidance, and a setup script for new projects.
skill.summary: Apply Vitest and TypeScript patterns to write tests first, implement behavior, and refactor with confidence.
skill.author: Emz1998

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many command, filesystem, and weak-crypto patterns, but most Markdown hits are false positives from TypeScript and Vitest examples. The executable setup script is a real medium-risk component because it runs npm commands and writes files based on a user-supplied project path, but I found no evidence of malicious intent, network exfiltration, or prompt injection.

medium_findings.title: Setup Script Runs Package Manager Commands
medium_findings.description: The setup script imports child_process and calls execSync through a helper to run npm init and install Vitest dependencies. The commands are hardcoded, so command injection is not confirmed, but running package-manager commands can execute dependency and lifecycle behavior in the target project.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct code evidence shows child_process.execSync runs hardcoded npm commands. The usage appears legitimate for project setup, but it still creates a real execution risk.

medium_findings.title: User-Controlled Project Path Affects File Writes
medium_findings.description: The project name comes from process.argv[2], is used to create and enter a directory, and then the script writes project files. This is expected for a scaffolding script, but there is no path normalization or restriction to prevent writes outside the intended workspace.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The argv value directly controls mkdirSync and process.chdir before file creation. This is a legitimate setup feature, but the missing path guard makes the risk credible.

low_findings.title: Markdown Static Hits Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The numerous weak-crypto, Ruby backtick, and reconnaissance reports in SKILL.md and reference Markdown files are from code fences and testing prose. I found no evidence that these examples execute commands, access secrets, or attempt prompt injection.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown examples for Vitest and TypeScript, not executable skill logic. A targeted prompt-injection search also found no suspicious override language.

dangerous_patterns.title: Synchronous External Command Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The script wraps execSync and uses it to invoke npm commands during setup. This pattern should be disclosed because it executes external tooling on the user machine.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The execSync helper and npm command calls are explicit. The commands are not dynamically built from user input, which lowers the severity.

dangerous_patterns.title: Project File Creation and Overwrite Behavior
dangerous_patterns.description: The script writes tsconfig.json, vitest.config.ts, source files, tests, package.json, README.md, and optionally .gitignore. This is normal scaffolding behavior, but it can modify an existing project when run in the current directory.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file writes are directly visible in the setup script. The purpose is legitimate scaffolding, but users should know it modifies project files.

content.user_title: Build Vitest TDD Workflows for TypeScript
content.value_statement: TypeScript teams often struggle to keep tests focused, fast, and useful during feature work. This skill guides Vitest-based TDD with setup help, patterns, anti-patterns, and refactoring discipline.
actual_capabilities: Guides red-green-refactor workflows for TypeScript features.
Provides Vitest configuration patterns for Node and coverage reports.
Shows unit, integration, async, mock, and error-handling test examples.
Explains testing anti-patterns and safer alternatives.
Includes a setup script that can scaffold a Vitest TypeScript project.
limitations: It does not run tests unless the agent or user invokes commands.
The setup script can modify project files and install dependencies.
Examples are generic and may need adaptation for specific frameworks.
It does not replace human review of test quality or product behavior.

use_cases.title: Start a Tested Feature
use_cases.description: Write the first failing Vitest test, implement minimal TypeScript behavior, and refactor after the test passes.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Testing
use_cases.description: Create consistent test structure, naming conventions, coverage expectations, and anti-pattern guidance for a TypeScript codebase.

use_cases.title: Improve Existing Tests
use_cases.description: Review flaky, over-mocked, or implementation-focused tests and convert them into behavior-focused Vitest coverage.

prompt_templates.title: Write a First Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me use TDD for this TypeScript function. First write one failing Vitest test for the expected behavior, then explain the minimal implementation.

prompt_templates.title: Add Edge Cases
prompt_templates.scenario: Expand coverage after the happy path
prompt_templates.prompt: Review these Vitest tests and suggest edge cases, error cases, and clearer test names. Keep each test focused on one behavior.

prompt_templates.title: Refactor Test Design
prompt_templates.prompt: Refactor this TypeScript test file to reduce setup noise, avoid implementation details, and keep mocks limited to external dependencies.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Integration Coverage
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Vitest integration testing strategy for this TypeScript service. Include data setup, isolation, async behavior, failure cases, and coverage boundaries.

output_examples.input: I need to add a calculator divide feature with TDD.
output_examples.output: A focused failing test for division, a minimal implementation plan, and an added zero-division error test before refactoring.

output_examples.input: My Vitest tests are flaky because they wait with timers.
output_examples.output: A review that replaces fixed delays with condition-based waiting or explicit promises, plus notes on test isolation.

output_examples.input: Set up Vitest in a TypeScript project.
output_examples.output: A setup checklist covering dependencies, Vitest configuration, TypeScript test types, scripts, and a safe reminder before running the scaffold script.

best_practices: Start with a failing test that describes one observable behavior.
Mock only external dependencies and keep business logic under real tests.
Refactor after tests pass while keeping the suite fast and deterministic.
anti_patterns: Do not test private implementation details instead of public behavior.
Do not use arbitrary sleeps or timers to hide async timing issues.
Do not run the setup script in a valuable project without reviewing file changes.

faq.question: Does this skill only work with Vitest?
faq.answer: Its examples and setup focus on Vitest, but many TDD practices apply to other TypeScript test runners.

faq.question: Can it create a new test project?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a setup script that creates configuration, sample source files, tests, scripts, and documentation.

faq.question: Is the setup script required?
faq.answer: No. The guidance can be used manually without running the script.

faq.question: What should I review before running the script?
faq.answer: Review the target directory, npm install behavior, and generated file names because the script writes project files.

faq.question: Does it cover integration tests?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for multi-unit flows, databases, async behavior, contracts, and error recovery.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, or Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
